title: Agency Handlers & Patrons
description: Who is giving the crew its jobs and what they want — their manner, their leverage, and the shape of their agenda. Roll for the figure on the other side of the table when the crew is briefed.

entries.result: A Ministry archivist who meets in tea rooms and pretends each job is an academic favor. Speaks in the passive voice. The leverage is filed in a drawer they have not yet opened, and they want it kept closed.
A canal-merchant prince who runs a shipping concern as cover for older interests. Polite to the point of insult. Pays in coin and information and expects the crew to know which is the larger sum.

A widowed magistrate's wife who inherited her husband's debts and his contacts, and has discovered she preferred the contacts. Treats every meeting like court protocol and every betrayal like an annulment.

A defrocked priest who runs a legitimate notary business and an illegitimate one in the back. Knows the binding-prayers and the binding-clauses both. Wants something a former superior keeps in a locked cabinet.
An anonymous voice on a brass speaking-tube in a particular alley. Calls every Tuesday at midnight. Has never given a name and has never been wrong about a payout, which is somehow worse.
A retired field agent of the Ministry, drinking themselves slowly to death in a flat above a tobacconist. Their information is two decades stale and twice as good as the current generation's.
A spirit-court magistrate, technically deceased, who holds office hours in a sealed reading-room of the public library. Their jobs always have a clause the crew misses on first reading.
A textile-mill heiress whose family business has bankrolled three insurrections and recouped all losses. Treats jobs as portfolio diversification. Expects detailed quarterly reports.

A dockside fixer with no surname who has been forty-seven years old for at least three decades. Pays in advance, never haggles, never explains. The few crews who tried to follow them home are still missing.
A clockmaker's widow who inherited a workshop and a clientele. Continues both businesses without distinguishing between them. Will not work with anyone who arrives late.
A rising politician whose campaign requires certain rivals' careers to be ended quietly and certain skeletons to remain in particular closets. Treats the crew as staff and bristles at familiarity.

An exiled aristocrat from a small inland country, residing in a hotel suite they have not left in eleven years. The hotel does not bill them and the staff does not remember why. They are funding a slow, patient revenge.
A spirit bound into a porcelain doll kept in a velvet case by a mortal proxy. The proxy speaks; the doll listens. The crew is hired by the doll and paid by the proxy and the proxy resents this.
A Ministry inspector who is technically still on duty and is technically not authorized to be doing this. Pays from a budget line item labeled 'cleaning supplies.' Will deny everything if asked.
A bookseller specializing in occult treatises who is also the unofficial broker for three rival cults' off-the-books operations. Maintains strict neutrality and excellent records.

A masked figure who meets the crew only in a particular gondola at a particular hour. The gondolier does not speak. The mask is the same mask each time but the voice behind it is not always the same person.

A gentlewoman of indeterminate age who runs a salon for the city's intellectual underclass. Funds the crew through a charity for orphaned scholars. The orphaned scholars are real and have no idea where the funding comes from.
A union steward for the canal-workers who has discovered the union's pension fund can be put to many uses. The workers do not know. The patron-spirit of the canals is beginning to suspect.
A society medium famous for séances at the right addresses. The séances are real. The clients are also clients of the crew, though only the medium knows it.
A consortium of three brothers who operate a respectable import firm and meet the crew only in rotating pairs. Each brother believes the other two are the principals. They are not.

A Ministry archivist's clerk — not the archivist — who has been quietly running half their superior's caseload for years and pocketing the difference. Terrified of being noticed by either direction of the hierarchy.
A retired sea captain whose ship was condemned, sunk, and is still occasionally seen in the harbor. They live in a boarding house and pay the crew in shares of the ship's continuing cargo.
A bone-setter and back-alley physician whose clients include several names from the noble registry. Hires the crew for jobs the clients are too well-connected to ask anyone respectable about.
A cathedral organist who has been playing in the same church for decades and has not aged a day in fifteen years. The bishop knows. The bishop is also, separately, a client.
An ironmonger whose foundry produces nails, hinges, and exactly four sets of binding-irons per year by special commission. The foundry's actual revenue cannot be explained by nails alone.

A boarding-school headmistress who runs the crew the way she runs her institution: with timetables, demerits, and unannounced inspections. Pays bonuses for clean penmanship in the after-action reports.

A theater impresario whose productions are wildly successful and whose backstage employs an unusual number of former soldiers, surgeons, and locksmiths. The plays are real; the players are also assets.
A widower who manages an orphanage with quiet dignity and absolute cruelty. The orphanage is funded entirely by the crew's work. The orphans, when they age out, are placed in unusual professions.

A junior diplomat from a foreign legation who is running an unauthorized private operation in defiance of their ambassador. Pays in foreign currency and embassy gossip. Will be recalled at any time and knows it.
A ghost still drawing a Ministry pension through a clerical error and using the money to settle old grievances. Meets the crew through a living intermediary and signs nothing personally.
A leviathan-oil refiner who sits on the city merchants' council and has been quietly buying up rival operations through cutouts. The crew is one of the cutouts. They were not informed.

A Ministry exorcist whose career path involves removing things from places, and who has begun to find that the same skills work for non-supernatural removal. Ethically conflicted and absolutely committed.
A horticulturalist whose greenhouse on the city's edge produces certain unlisted varieties for certain interested parties. The plants require unusual fertilizer; the crew sometimes provides it.
A barrister who specializes in trust law and the dissolution of inconvenient family arrangements. Hires the crew for the inconveniences that cannot be addressed in chambers.
A wax-cylinder recording engineer whose archive includes the voices of the dead, the indiscreet, and the supposedly silent. The archive is the leverage. The recordings keep growing.
A society astrologer who genuinely sees the future but cannot do anything about most of what they see. Hires the crew to make small interventions in the patterns. Pays in horoscopes and warnings.
A pawnbroker whose shop window has rotated the same dozen items for fifteen years. The stock in back is quite different. Several items are owed back to clients who can never collect.
A Ministry sub-secretary so colorless that they have been overlooked for promotion for two decades. They have used the time productively. They have a list. The list is alphabetical.
A stage hypnotist who tours the music halls and uses the act as cover for the real work, which is conducted in private suites between performances. Genuinely good at the act.

A child of perhaps eleven who is seated in a high-backed chair when the crew arrives, drinking warm milk, and who issues instructions in fluent multiple languages. No one in the household will explain.

A fishmonger at the canal market whose stall is run by a nephew while she manages other affairs from a back office. The nephew thinks she is engaged in a long property dispute. He is not entirely wrong.
A locksmith's guildmaster whose official duties include vetting the city's master keys and whose unofficial duties include occasionally arranging for the wrong people to have the right ones.
An anonymous benefactor whose instructions arrive via banker's draft, registered post, and once, alarmingly, embedded in a song the crew heard in three separate taverns over a single evening.
A war veteran turned tobacconist whose shop has a back room used for meetings of a particular old comrades' association. The association meets to settle accounts the war did not.
A spirit-medium married into a respectable family who uses the marriage as legal cover and the gifts as professional tools. Her husband knows about half of it and prefers not to learn the rest.

A demolition contractor with an unusual specialty in determining which buildings the city would benefit from losing, and arranging convenient gas-line accidents to that end. Fully bonded and licensed.

An imported dog of a particular breed who is, in fact, the patron — bound through an ancient contract that displaced a previous handler. The handler the crew speaks to is a kennel-keeper who interprets the dog.
A Ministry mortician whose access to recent corpses, their effects, and their last appointments has made them one of the better-informed people in the city. Pays in tobacco and exclusive information.
A railway clerk who arranges for certain crates to travel on certain trains and certain inspectors to look elsewhere. The railway is a cover; what they actually move is information.
A salon-keeper whose Thursday gatherings include politicians, criminals, mediums, and the occasional foreign agent. They take no side and run errands for all sides simultaneously.
A physician at the workhouse infirmary who has access to the unclaimed dead and the deeply indebted living. Hires the crew for jobs requiring discretion about either category.

A dancer at a particular cabaret who is also the principal cipher-clerk for a syndicate that does not officially exist. Conducts briefings during her dressing-room turnaround. Pays from a strongbox under the makeup table.

A retired judge living modestly in a small house with a very large garden and a very small staff. The staff are former criminals the judge sentenced and quietly arranged to release. They are loyal to her, not to the law.

A newspaperman whose paper publishes the official version and whose office files preserve every other version. The crew is occasionally hired to make sure the gap between the two does not become evidence.

A foreign trade attaché whose actual portfolio is local intelligence, and who has been quietly running operations in this city for so long that they now consider it home and resent newer arrivals from the home country.
A ghost-broker who arranges introductions between the living and the recently deceased for purposes ranging from inheritance disputes to confessions. Hires the crew when an introduction goes badly.
A coffee-house proprietor whose establishment is the unofficial waiting room for at least three different organizations' freelance work. They take no fee and are paid handsomely by all parties.

A society photographer whose portrait subjects include the city's notable families. The portraits are masterful. The proof negatives, kept separately, occasionally reveal things the subject would rather not have recorded.

A Ministry clerk who was demoted to records-room duty as a punishment, has been there fourteen years, and has used the time to construct a private intelligence apparatus that rivals their old department.

A figure who only ever appears as an empty chair at the meeting, with instructions delivered through whichever crew member is closest to the chair at the time. The instructions are clear, consistent, and never wrong.
